symptom: a slowly enlarging neck with breathlessness and trouble swallowing when lying flat
hook: a big soft tissue mass was pushing down from the neck into the top of the chest and shoving the windpipe sideways
keyFindings: a superior mediastinal mass continuous with the neck that deviates and narrows the trachea and may extend below the clavicles
whatYouSee: A superior mediastinal mass extending up into the neck that pushes the trachea to one side and narrows it.
whyItMatters: An enlarged thyroid has grown down behind the breastbone where it can compress the trachea and the oesophagus and the great vessels.
treatment: Treatment is surgical removal when it compresses nearby structures or keeps growing.
takeaway: A neck mass diving behind the breastbone and shoving the trachea is a retrosternal goiter.

symptom: a steadily swelling abdomen mistaken for weight gain or pregnancy
hook: a single enormous fluid filled balloon was filling almost the whole abdomen and pushing the bowel aside
keyFindings: a huge well defined soft tissue or fluid density mass filling the abdomen and displacing the bowel loops to the periphery
whatYouSee: A huge smooth mass filling most of the abdomen and pushing the bowel gas out to the edges.
whyItMatters: A benign ovarian cyst can grow to fill the whole abdomen and most are benign but a few hide a cancer so it always needs assessment.
treatment: Treatment is surgical removal with histology to exclude malignancy.
takeaway: A giant smooth mass filling the abdomen and shoving bowel to the sides is a massive ovarian cyst.

symptom: short stature with bones that fractured easily and a small receding jaw
hook: the bones were strikingly dense and white yet the fingertips looked dissolved away at the very tips
keyFindings: diffusely dense sclerotic bones with acro-osteolysis of the distal phalanges plus open skull sutures and a small obtuse mandible
whatYouSee: Uniformly dense white bones with the fingertip bones partly dissolved and an underdeveloped jaw.
whyItMatters: It is an inherited sclerosing bone disease that makes bone dense yet brittle so fractures are common despite all the density.
treatment: Care is supportive with fracture management and dental and orthopaedic follow up.
takeaway: Dense brittle bones with dissolved fingertips and a small jaw point to pycnodysostosis.

symptom: multiple hard painless lumps on the skull and jaw with a strong family history of bowel polyps
hook: several dense bony knobs were studded across the skull and jaw like little stones
keyFindings: multiple dense well defined osteomas of the skull and facial bones and mandible often with impacted or extra teeth
whatYouSee: Several dense rounded bony growths on the skull and jaw.
whyItMatters: These osteomas flag Gardner syndrome which carries near certain colon cancer from numerous polyps so spotting them prompts life saving bowel screening.
treatment: The osteomas are removed only if symptomatic but the key step is urgent colorectal screening and management of the polyps.
takeaway: Multiple bony knobs on the skull and jaw can be Gardner syndrome which demands bowel cancer screening.

symptom: an irritable infant with hard tender swelling along the jaw and limbs
hook: the baby's jaw and long bones were wrapped in thick new layers of bone like tree bark
keyFindings: florid cortical thickening and periosteal new bone classically of the mandible and clavicle and long bones in an infant under six months
whatYouSee: Thick layers of new bone coating the jaw and long bones in a young infant.
whyItMatters: It is a self limiting infantile disorder of exuberant new bone that can mimic infection or injury so recognising it avoids alarm and needless treatment.
treatment: It usually settles on its own and anti inflammatories help symptoms in severe cases.
takeaway: Thick new bone on the jaw and limbs of a young baby is Caffey disease.

symptom: leg pain a waddling walk and weak muscles in a child or young adult
hook: the shafts of the long bones were thickened and dense while the ends stayed normal
keyFindings: symmetric cortical thickening and sclerosis of the diaphyses of the long bones with sparing of the epiphyses and metaphyses
whatYouSee: Thickened dense midshafts of the long bones with the ends looking normal.
whyItMatters: It is an inherited bone dysplasia that thickens the shafts and causes bone pain and a waddling gait and can narrow the skull base.
treatment: Treatment is supportive and steroids are used for bone pain in some patients.
takeaway: Dense thickened long bone shafts with normal ends point to Engelmann disease.

symptom: a child with skull lumps excessive thirst and bulging eyes
hook: the skull had several map like holes punched clean through it with sharp edges
keyFindings: multiple well defined lytic skull lesions with a geographic map like outline and beveled edges from uneven inner and outer table loss
whatYouSee: Several sharply outlined map like holes in the skull bones.
whyItMatters: It is a form of Langerhans cell histiocytosis and the classic triad adds diabetes insipidus and bulging eyes so the skull holes flag a systemic disease.
treatment: Treatment ranges from observation to chemotherapy depending on how many systems are involved.
takeaway: Map like punched out skull holes in a thirsty child suggest Hand-Schuller-Christian disease.

symptom: a tall heavy boned person with facial swelling and progressive facial weakness and hearing loss
hook: every bone looked massively overgrown and dense especially the skull which had thickened enormously
keyFindings: marked symmetric bony overgrowth and sclerosis of the skull and mandible and tubular bones with narrowing of the skull foramina
whatYouSee: Greatly thickened dense skull and jaw bones with overgrown dense long bones.
whyItMatters: It is a rare sclerosing dysplasia from too much bone formation and the thickened skull traps the cranial nerves causing facial weakness and deafness and raised pressure.
treatment: Care is supportive with surgery to decompress trapped nerves or the brainstem when needed.
takeaway: A massively dense overgrown skull with trapped facial nerves points to sclerosteosis.

symptom: swollen sausage like fingers in someone with known lung sarcoidosis
hook: the small bones of the fingers had a delicate lace like honeycomb pattern eaten into them
keyFindings: lace like trabecular resorption and well defined lytic cysts in the phalanges with soft tissue swelling and preserved joint spaces
whatYouSee: A delicate lace like pattern and small round holes in the finger bones with swollen soft tissue.
whyItMatters: Bone sarcoid usually appears with chronic systemic sarcoidosis so the lacy phalanges are a clue to wider disease in the lungs and elsewhere.
treatment: Treatment is of the underlying sarcoidosis with steroids or other immune therapy when active.
takeaway: A lace like honeycomb pattern in swollen fingers points to bone sarcoidosis.

symptom: progressive lopsided overgrowth since childhood with one grotesquely enlarged foot and giant toes
hook: one foot had overgrown grotesquely with massively enlarged bones and giant toes
view: dorsoplantar (AP) radiograph of a single grossly enlarged foot cropped at the ankle with no leg pelvis or groin in frame and one view filling the frame
keyFindings: gross macrodactyly with one or more massively overgrown giant toes two to three times the normal size and greatly enlarged distorted metatarsals and phalanges giving a monstrously enlarged misshapen foot
whatYouSee: A wildly enlarged distorted foot with giant overgrown toes.
whyItMatters: It is a rare mosaic overgrowth disorder that enlarges bones and tissues progressively and asymmetrically and can cause severe deformity and complications.
treatment: Care is supportive and orthopaedic with surgery to debulk or correct disabling overgrowth.
takeaway: Progressive lopsided overgrowth with giant distorted bones and digits suggests Proteus syndrome.

symptom: profound hearing loss treated with a surgically placed hearing device
hook: a tight little coil of wire was spiralling into the inner ear like a watch spring
keyFindings: a coiled radiopaque electrode array spiralling into the cochlea with a receiver-stimulator package fixed to the skull behind the ear and a lead running to it
whatYouSee: A fine coiled wire spiralling into the cochlea with a small electronic receiver fixed to the skull behind the ear.
whyItMatters: It is a surgically implanted device that restores hearing by stimulating the cochlear nerve so recognising it avoids mistaking the coil for a foreign body or lesion.
treatment: No treatment is needed for the device itself; it is programmed by the audiology team and MRI safety must be checked first.
takeaway: A tiny wire coil spiralling into the inner ear with a skull receiver is a cochlear implant.

symptom: crampy abdominal pain and vomiting in an older patient with known gallstones
hook: the bowel had ballooned with gas and a stray stone had wandered far from the gallbladder
keyFindings: the Rigler triad of dilated gas-filled small bowel loops plus gas in the biliary tree and an ectopic gallstone lodged in the bowel
whatYouSee: Dilated small bowel loops with air outlining the bile ducts and a single stray gallstone sitting low in the abdomen.
whyItMatters: A large gallstone has eroded through into the gut and lodged in the small bowel causing mechanical obstruction and it mostly strikes older patients and is easy to miss.
treatment: Treatment is surgical removal of the obstructing stone with later attention to the gallbladder fistula.
takeaway: Bowel obstruction with air in the bile ducts and a stray stone is gallstone ileus.

symptom: a chest that visibly caved inward at the breastbone since the teenage years
hook: the heart looked shoved to the left and the front of the chest seemed to vanish into a hollow
keyFindings: a depressed sternum pushing the heart to the left with a blurred right heart border on the frontal film and an obvious posterior sternal depression on the lateral
whatYouSee: The breastbone is sunken inward which shifts the heart to the left and blurs the right heart border.
whyItMatters: It is the most common chest wall deformity and is usually cosmetic but a severe funnel can compress the heart and lungs and reduce exercise tolerance.
treatment: Mild cases are observed; significant deformity is corrected surgically with a bar that lifts the sternum.
takeaway: A sunken breastbone shifting the heart left with a blurred right heart border is pectus excavatum.

symptom: a swollen deformed foot that strangely did not hurt in a patient with long-standing diabetes
hook: the joint looked like a bag of crushed bone fragments yet the patient felt almost nothing
keyFindings: gross joint destruction with fragmentation and bony debris and dense sclerosis and dislocation and disorganisation of the midfoot in a neuropathic patient
whatYouSee: A destroyed disorganised joint full of bone fragments with collapse and dislocation of the midfoot.
whyItMatters: Loss of protective sensation lets repeated trauma destroy the joint painlessly which is classic in diabetes and risks ulceration and amputation if missed.
treatment: Treatment is offloading and immobilisation with protected footwear and surgery is reserved for severe instability or deformity.
takeaway: A painlessly destroyed disorganised joint in a patient with neuropathy is a Charcot joint.

symptom: lumpy, deformed fingers that had grown over years
hook: the hand was studded with bubbly bone lumps and little round stones in the soft tissue
keyFindings: multiple expansile lucent enchondromas in the phalanges and metacarpals with endosteal scalloping, plus rounded soft-tissue phleboliths from the hemangiomas
whatYouSee: Multiple lytic, expansile enchondromas in the small bones of the hand alongside round, calcified phleboliths sitting in the soft tissues.
whyItMatters: It is a non-hereditary combination of multiple enchondromas and soft-tissue hemangiomas that carries a markedly raised lifetime risk of malignant transformation, especially chondrosarcoma.
treatment: There is no cure; management is surveillance for malignant change, correction of deformity or fractures, and excision of any lesion that grows or becomes painful.
takeaway: Bone lumps plus little round stones in the soft tissue of the hand point to Maffucci syndrome, which needs lifelong cancer watch.

symptom: one arm and hand that had grown shorter and more crooked than the other
hook: the bones looked like they were stuffed with translucent cartilage balloons, mostly on one side
keyFindings: multiple expansile lucent enchondromas with a typically unilateral or asymmetric distribution, causing bone expansion, deformity and shortening, often with linear striations
whatYouSee: Numerous lucent, expansile cartilage lesions clustered asymmetrically in the bones of one limb, distorting and shortening it.
whyItMatters: This is multiple enchondromatosis without the hemangiomas of Maffucci; it causes limb deformity and length discrepancy and carries a real risk of chondrosarcoma.
treatment: Management is orthopaedic correction of deformity and length discrepancy plus long-term monitoring for malignant transformation.
takeaway: Many cartilage lesions crowding mostly one side of the body is Ollier disease, the hemangioma-free cousin of Maffucci.

symptom: a shoulder that sat too high and could not lift the arm fully since birth
hook: one shoulder blade was parked up near the neck instead of lying flat on the back
keyFindings: a small, abnormally elevated and medially rotated scapula, sometimes connected to the cervical spine by an omovertebral bone
whatYouSee: One scapula sits abnormally high and is smaller and rotated, occasionally bridged to the cervical spine by an extra bone.
whyItMatters: It is the most common congenital shoulder anomaly and limits shoulder abduction; it is frequently associated with Klippel-Feil and other spine anomalies.
treatment: Mild cases are observed; significant functional or cosmetic deformity is corrected surgically, ideally in early childhood.
takeaway: A shoulder blade riding high near the neck from birth is the Sprengel deformity.
igTitle: THE SHOULDER THAT WOULD NOT DROP

symptom: a stabbing throat and neck pain that got worse on turning the head and swallowing
hook: a long bony spike was reaching down from the skull base toward the throat
keyFindings: an abnormally elongated styloid process (greater than about 3 cm) and/or calcification of the stylohyoid ligament projecting toward the tonsillar fossa
whatYouSee: A long, slender, ossified spike extending from the skull base down toward the throat, far longer than the normal styloid process.
whyItMatters: The elongated styloid or calcified ligament can irritate nearby nerves and vessels, causing throat pain, referred ear pain, and pain on neck movement.
treatment: Symptoms can be managed conservatively with analgesics or local injections; refractory cases are treated by surgical shortening of the styloid process.
takeaway: A bony spike pointing into the throat with neck and swallow pain is Eagle syndrome.

symptom: no symptoms at all; the spots turned up by chance on an X-ray
hook: the bone ends were sprayed with dozens of tiny dense white dots like a snowstorm
keyFindings: numerous small, round-to-oval sclerotic foci clustered symmetrically in the epiphyses and metaphyses around joints, especially pelvis, hips and hands
whatYouSee: Dozens of small, well-defined dense white spots scattered symmetrically through the bone ends around the joints.
whyItMatters: It is a benign, often inherited bone dysplasia that is clinically harmless but is important to recognise so it is not mistaken for blastic metastases.
treatment: No treatment is needed; the key is confident recognition to avoid unnecessary cancer work-up.
takeaway: A harmless snowstorm of tiny dense dots in the bone ends is osteopoikilosis, not cancer.

symptom: a stiff, aching limb with thickened skin overlying the bone
hook: dense bone was dripping down the side of the shaft like candle wax
keyFindings: flowing, wavy cortical hyperostosis along one side of a long bone resembling melted candle wax, usually in a single limb and sclerotomal distribution
whatYouSee: Thick, flowing sclerotic bone running down one side of the shaft like wax dripping from a candle.
whyItMatters: This rare benign dysplasia can cause pain, stiffness, limb deformity and soft-tissue contractures along the affected segment.
treatment: Management is symptomatic with analgesia and physiotherapy; severe deformity or contracture may need surgical release or correction.
takeaway: Bone that looks like dripping candle wax down one side of a limb is melorheostosis.

symptom: soft tissues that kept turning hard and locking the joints, with short malformed big toes
hook: sheets of new bone were forming inside the muscles, fusing the body into a second skeleton
keyFindings: heterotopic ossification forming bony bridges within muscles and along soft-tissue planes, classically with short malformed great toes (hallux valgus / monophalangism)
whatYouSee: Ribbons and sheets of bone forming inside the muscles and across soft tissues, with characteristically short, malformed big toes.
whyItMatters: This ultra-rare genetic disorder progressively turns soft tissue into bone, locking joints; trauma and biopsy famously trigger more bone, so they must be avoided.
treatment: There is no cure; care focuses on avoiding trauma and unnecessary surgery, managing flares, and protecting remaining mobility.
takeaway: New bone growing inside the muscles with short malformed big toes is FOP, the stone man syndrome.

symptom: long-standing right upper abdominal discomfort and bloating
hook: it looked like someone had hidden a giant laminated pearl inside the abdomen
keyFindings: a single very large rounded calculus in the right upper quadrant, often with concentric laminated calcification or a central lucency
whatYouSee: A large rounded calcified stone in the right upper quadrant, sometimes with layered, onion-ring calcification.
whyItMatters: Giant gallstones can cause chronic cholecystitis and, rarely, erode into bowel to produce gallstone ileus, a mechanical obstruction.
treatment: Symptomatic stones are treated by cholecystectomy; gallstone ileus also requires relief of the bowel obstruction.
takeaway: A single huge layered stone in the right upper abdomen is a giant gallstone.

symptom: recurrent urinary infections and dull flank ache
hook: a stone had grown into a perfect cast of the kidney's inner branches, like antlers
keyFindings: a large branching calcified density filling and outlining the renal pelvis and calyces in the shape of a stag's antlers
whatYouSee: A dense branching stone that fills the collecting system of the kidney, shaped like deer antlers.
whyItMatters: Staghorn calculi are usually infection (struvite) stones that can destroy renal function and serve as a persistent nidus for infection and sepsis.
treatment: Definitive treatment is surgical clearance, classically percutaneous nephrolithotomy, plus treatment of the underlying infection.
takeaway: A stone shaped like antlers filling the kidney's collecting system is a staghorn calculus.

symptom: a hugely distended abdomen with no bowel movements and crampy pain
hook: a massive loop of bowel had twisted up into the shape of a giant coffee bean
keyFindings: a markedly dilated inverted-U loop of sigmoid colon rising out of the pelvis, with a central cleft giving the classic coffee-bean appearance pointing toward the right upper quadrant
whatYouSee: A grossly dilated loop of large bowel folded on itself, looking like a giant coffee bean rising from the pelvis.
whyItMatters: The twisted segment can obstruct and lose its blood supply, leading to ischaemia, perforation and peritonitis if not relieved.
treatment: Initial treatment is endoscopic decompression and detorsion; recurrent or complicated cases need sigmoid colectomy.
takeaway: A giant coffee-bean loop of bowel from the pelvis is a sigmoid volvulus.

symptom: sudden severe abdominal pain with a rigid, board-like belly
hook: a thin black crescent of free air was trapped under the dome of the diaphragm
keyFindings: free gas under the diaphragm on an erect film, seen as a lucent crescent between the diaphragm and liver or stomach
whatYouSee: A crescent of black free air sitting under the diaphragm, separate from the normal stomach gas bubble.
whyItMatters: Free intraperitoneal air usually signals a perforated hollow organ, a surgical emergency that can rapidly cause peritonitis and sepsis.
treatment: Treatment is urgent resuscitation and, in most cases, emergency surgery to find and repair the perforation.
takeaway: A crescent of free air under the diaphragm means a perforated organ until proven otherwise.
igTitle: AIR WHERE IT SHOULD NOT BE

symptom: years of difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids, with regurgitation
hook: the food pipe had ballooned wide and tapered to a thin bird's beak at the bottom
keyFindings: a dilated oesophagus with smooth tapering to a narrow point at the gastro-oesophageal junction producing the classic bird-beak appearance, often with retained food and an air-fluid level
whatYouSee: A widely dilated oesophagus that narrows smoothly to a thin point at the bottom, like a bird's beak.
whyItMatters: It is failure of the lower oesophageal sphincter to relax with loss of peristalsis, causing progressive dysphagia, weight loss and aspiration risk.
treatment: Treatment options include pneumatic dilatation, peroral endoscopic myotomy or surgical Heller myotomy to relieve the obstruction.
takeaway: A dilated oesophagus tapering to a bird's beak is achalasia.

symptom: no symptoms; discovered incidentally on a routine chest film
hook: the heart was pointing to the right and the stomach bubble sat under the wrong lung
keyFindings: the cardiac apex and aortic arch on the right (dextrocardia) with the gastric air bubble under the right hemidiaphragm and liver shadow on the left, a complete mirror-image of normal anatomy
whatYouSee: The heart points to the right and the stomach gas bubble and organs are all flipped to the opposite side, a mirror image of normal.
whyItMatters: It is usually benign, but recognising it prevents diagnostic errors and missed lateralised disease; with primary ciliary dyskinesia it forms Kartagener syndrome.
treatment: No treatment is required for the mirror anatomy itself; associated conditions such as Kartagener syndrome are managed on their own.
takeaway: A heart pointing right with flipped organs is situs inversus, a mirror-image body.

symptom: cough, breathlessness and weight loss in someone with a known cancer
hook: both lungs were peppered with large round shadows like a stack of cannonballs
keyFindings: multiple large, well-defined round nodules scattered through both lungs, typically from renal, choriocarcinoma, or other distant primary tumours
whatYouSee: Multiple large, round, sharply marginated masses spread through both lung fields like cannonballs.
whyItMatters: These represent haematogenous metastatic spread to the lungs and indicate advanced malignancy requiring identification of the primary tumour.
treatment: Management depends on the primary cancer and may include systemic chemotherapy, targeted therapy or immunotherapy.
takeaway: Big round shadows scattered through both lungs are cannonball metastases.

symptom: a long-forgotten failed pregnancy years earlier with vague abdominal fullness
hook: a calcified fetus had been sitting silently in the abdomen for years, turned to stone
keyFindings: a calcified fetal skeleton lying within the abdomen or pelvis outside the uterine cavity, with recognisable calcified fetal bones
whatYouSee: The calcified outline of a fetal skeleton lying free in the abdomen or pelvis, long after a failed pregnancy.
whyItMatters: It is a rare end-stage of an abdominal or ectopic pregnancy where the dead fetus calcifies instead of being reabsorbed, and can be mistaken for a tumour or stone.
treatment: Management is usually surgical removal, though asymptomatic cases in frail patients may be observed.
takeaway: A calcified fetus retained for years in the abdomen is a lithopedion, a stone baby.

symptom: vague abdominal pain and a mass months after an operation
hook: a swirling mass with a tell-tale radiopaque marker was a sponge left behind in surgery
keyFindings: a soft-tissue mass containing a whorled or spongiform gas pattern, often with a visible radiopaque marker thread from a retained surgical sponge
whatYouSee: A soft-tissue mass with a swirled internal pattern and, often, a thin radiopaque marker line from a retained surgical sponge.
whyItMatters: A retained sponge can cause chronic infection, abscess and fistula or mimic a tumour, and is a serious preventable surgical complication.
treatment: Treatment is surgical removal of the retained material and management of any abscess or fistula.
takeaway: A swirled abdominal mass with a radiopaque marker after surgery is a gossypiboma, a left-behind sponge.

symptom: long-standing one-sided blocked nose and foul-smelling discharge
hook: a hard calcified stone had quietly built up inside one nostril over years
keyFindings: a dense, often irregular calcified mass within the nasal cavity, typically unilateral on the floor of the nose
whatYouSee: A dense, irregular calcified mass sitting inside one side of the nasal cavity.
whyItMatters: A rhinolith forms by mineral deposition around a retained foreign body or debris and causes chronic unilateral obstruction, discharge and odour.
treatment: Treatment is endoscopic or surgical removal of the stone.
takeaway: A calcified stone inside one nostril causing chronic blockage is a rhinolith.

symptom: tingling and weakness in the hand worsened by raising the arm
hook: an extra rib was sprouting from the lowest neck vertebra where none should exist
keyFindings: a supernumerary rib arising from the C7 vertebra, ranging from a small bony stub to a complete extra rib above the first thoracic rib
whatYouSee: An extra rib growing from the seventh cervical vertebra, above the normal first rib.
whyItMatters: A cervical rib can compress the brachial plexus or subclavian vessels, causing thoracic outlet syndrome with neurological or vascular symptoms in the arm.
treatment: Mild cases are managed with physiotherapy; significant nerve or vascular compression is treated by surgical resection of the rib.
takeaway: An extra rib off the C7 vertebra is a cervical rib, a classic cause of thoracic outlet syndrome.
igTitle: THE RIB THAT SHOULD NOT BE

symptom: breathing difficulty with bowel sounds heard in the chest
hook: loops of bowel had climbed up through a hole in the diaphragm into the chest
keyFindings: gas-filled bowel loops within the thorax, usually on the left, through a posterolateral diaphragmatic defect, with shift of the mediastinum away from the herniated side
whatYouSee: Bowel loops sitting up inside the chest cavity through a defect in the back of the diaphragm, pushing the heart to the other side.
whyItMatters: This congenital diaphragmatic defect lets abdominal organs into the chest, compressing the developing lung and impairing breathing, especially in newborns.
treatment: Treatment is surgical repair of the diaphragmatic defect after stabilisation of breathing.
takeaway: Bowel loops in the chest through a back-of-diaphragm hole is a Bochdalek hernia.

symptom: repeated fractures from minor knocks since early childhood, with bowed limbs
hook: the bones were paper-thin and bowed, marked with healed fractures at every turn
keyFindings: diffusely thin, osteopenic, gracile bones with multiple fractures at different healing stages, bowing deformities and thin cortices
whatYouSee: Thin, under-mineralised, bowed bones with multiple fractures in different stages of healing.
whyItMatters: It is an inherited collagen disorder causing fragile bones, blue sclerae and hearing loss, with fractures from minimal trauma.
treatment: Management includes bisphosphonates to strengthen bone, fracture care, surgical rodding of long bones, and physiotherapy.
takeaway: Thin, bowed bones with many old fractures point to osteogenesis imperfecta, brittle bone disease.

symptom: disproportionately short limbs with a normal-length trunk since birth
hook: the spinal canal narrowed going down instead of widening, with squared-off pelvic wings
keyFindings: narrowing interpedicular distance descending the lumbar spine, squared iliac wings with champagne-glass pelvic inlet, short proximal long bones and trident hands
whatYouSee: The spacing of the lumbar spinal canal narrows as it goes down, with square pelvic wings and short, stubby long bones.
whyItMatters: It is the most common form of disproportionate short stature; the narrowing spinal canal predisposes to spinal stenosis and cord or nerve compression.
treatment: Care is supportive and orthopaedic, treating spinal stenosis, foramen magnum compression and limb issues as they arise.
takeaway: A lumbar canal that narrows going down with short limbs is achondroplasia.

symptom: an aching, enlarging bone and a hat that no longer fit
hook: the skull bones were thickened and fluffy, like cotton wool stuffed into the vault
keyFindings: thickened skull with mixed lytic and sclerotic cotton-wool patches, coarsened trabeculae and bone enlargement; in long bones a flame-shaped advancing lytic front
whatYouSee: Thickened, expanded skull bone with fluffy patches of dense and lucent bone, like cotton wool.
whyItMatters: Disordered bone remodelling produces enlarged, weak bone prone to deformity, fracture, deafness and, rarely, malignant transformation.
treatment: Active painful disease is treated with bisphosphonates, with surgery reserved for fractures, deformity or arthritis.
takeaway: A thickened skull with cotton-wool patches is Paget disease of bone.

symptom: a crooked, dinner-fork-shaped wrist and limited rotation in a young person
hook: the forearm bones had grown unevenly, bowing the wrist into a fork shape
keyFindings: premature fusion of the volar-ulnar distal radial physis causing a bowed, shortened radius, dorsal ulnar prominence and triangular carpus wedged between the radius and ulna
whatYouSee: A short, bowed radius with the ulna sticking out dorsally and the wrist bones wedged into a triangle, giving a dinner-fork shape.
whyItMatters: This growth-plate disorder, often linked to Leri-Weill dyschondrosteosis, causes wrist deformity, pain and reduced forearm rotation.
treatment: Management ranges from observation and physiotherapy to surgical correction with osteotomy or release of the abnormal tethering ligament.
takeaway: A dinner-fork wrist from an uneven, bowed forearm in a young person is Madelung deformity.

symptom: a child drooling and refusing food after swallowing something
hook: a perfectly round metal disc was lodged in the food pipe, facing flat to the camera
keyFindings: a round radiopaque metallic disc lying in the coronal plane (face-on) at the level of the thoracic inlet, distinguishing oesophageal from tracheal location
whatYouSee: A round, dense metallic disc sitting face-on in the oesophagus, typically at the thoracic inlet.
whyItMatters: A lodged coin can cause obstruction, drooling and aspiration; a button battery looks similar but has a double-ring halo and is a corrosive emergency.
treatment: An impacted oesophageal coin is removed endoscopically; a suspected button battery requires emergency removal because it burns tissue within hours.
takeaway: A round metal disc face-on in the food pipe is a swallowed coin, but always rule out a button battery.

symptom: chest discomfort after a sword swallowing performance
hook: a full length sword ran straight down the throat and chest into the stomach
view: AP chest and upper abdomen only cropped at the lower ribs with no pelvis or groin in frame
keyFindings: a long straight radiopaque sword blade extending from the neck down the midline of the chest along the path of the oesophagus into the stomach in the upper abdomen with otherwise normal chest and upper abdomen anatomy and no pelvis or groin in the frame
whatYouSee: A long straight metal blade running down the throat and chest into the stomach.
whyItMatters: Sword swallowing is a performance feat that can perforate the food pipe and cause a life threatening chest infection.
treatment: Most cases pass uneventfully but a perforation needs urgent surgery and antibiotics.
takeaway: A long straight blade running down the food pipe into the stomach is a swallowed sword.

diagnosis: Nail gun injury to the skull
symptom: a headache and a small scalp wound after a nail gun misfired at work
hook: a full construction nail was buried deep inside the skull
view: lateral skull radiograph one view filling the frame with no other body part
keyFindings: a single long radiopaque construction nail penetrating the cranial vault with its flat head outside the skull and its pointed tip lodged deep within the brain
whatYouSee: A long construction nail driven straight through the skull into the brain.
whyItMatters: A nail gun can drive a nail into the brain through a tiny entry wound so the injury is easily missed without an X-ray.
treatment: It needs urgent neurosurgery to remove the nail and control bleeding and infection.
takeaway: A radiopaque nail crossing the skull into the brain is a penetrating nail gun injury.

symptom: a blocked nose with crusting and a foul smell for months
hook: a fully formed tooth was growing inside the nasal cavity
view: Waters view of the facial bones and nasal cavity one view filling the frame
keyFindings: a single large and unmistakably tooth shaped radiopaque density with a clear pointed crown and root that is obviously a whole tooth sitting high inside the nasal cavity well above the upper tooth row so it clearly looks out of place
whatYouSee: A real tooth growing inside the nose.
whyItMatters: An ectopic tooth can erupt into the nose and cause blockage infection and a foul discharge until it is removed.
treatment: It is removed with a minor endoscopic procedure through the nose.
takeaway: A tooth shaped density inside the nasal cavity is an ectopic intranasal tooth.

symptom: a child who swallowed a handful of coins from a piggy bank
hook: the stomach was packed with a pile of swallowed coins
view: AP chest and upper abdomen cropped at the navel with no pelvis or groin in frame
keyFindings: a cluster of round radiopaque coins of varying sizes piled together in the stomach in the upper abdomen with normal surrounding anatomy and no pelvis or groin in the frame
whatYouSee: A pile of round coins stacked together inside the stomach.
whyItMatters: Most swallowed coins pass on their own but a coin stuck in the food pipe or a swallowed battery can burn through the wall and needs urgent removal.
treatment: Small coins in the stomach are watched and usually pass while a stuck coin is removed by endoscopy.
takeaway: A pile of round metal discs in the stomach is swallowed coins.

symptom: stomach pain after swallowing a light bulb on a dare
hook: a whole intact light bulb was sitting inside the stomach
view: AP chest and upper abdomen cropped at the navel with no pelvis or groin in frame
keyFindings: the outline of a whole intact light bulb with its rounded glass top and threaded metal base lying within the stomach in the upper abdomen with no pelvis or groin in the frame
whatYouSee: A whole light bulb sitting inside the stomach.
whyItMatters: A swallowed glass bulb can break and cut the gut so it often needs careful removal.
treatment: It is usually removed by endoscopy or surgery before it can shatter.
takeaway: The outline of a bulb in the stomach is a swallowed light bulb.

symptom: an infant drooling and refusing feeds after swallowing a safety pin
hook: an open safety pin was stuck in the food pipe with its point upward
view: AP chest and neck of an infant one view filling the frame with no pelvis or groin
keyFindings: an open safety pin lodged in the upper food pipe at the level of the neck or chest with its sharp point open and the spring clearly visible
whatYouSee: An open safety pin stuck in the food pipe.
whyItMatters: An open sharp pin can tear the food pipe so it must come out quickly.
treatment: It is removed urgently by endoscopy.
takeaway: An open safety pin in the food pipe is a swallowed pin needing urgent removal.

symptom: a fall onto a steel rod at a construction site
hook: a long steel rod had passed straight through the chest
view: AP chest one view filling the frame with no pelvis or groin
keyFindings: a long straight thick radiopaque metal rod passing through the chest cavity crossing the lung fields with the surrounding ribs and chest anatomy otherwise intact
whatYouSee: A long steel rod passing straight through the chest.
whyItMatters: An impaling rod is left in place until surgery because pulling it out can cause fatal bleeding.
treatment: It is removed in the operating room with the chest opened to control bleeding.
takeaway: A long metal rod crossing the chest is an impalement injury.

symptom: a rapidly swelling painful leg with a foul wound after an injury
hook: the muscles of the limb were full of spreading gas bubbles
view: AP radiograph of a lower leg one view filling the frame with no pelvis or groin
keyFindings: multiple dark gas bubbles and streaks of gas tracking through the soft tissues and muscle planes of the limb giving a feathery bubbly pattern with the bone intact
whatYouSee: Bubbles of gas spreading through the muscles of the limb.
whyItMatters: Gas in the muscle means a fast spreading flesh eating infection that can kill within hours.
treatment: It needs emergency surgery to cut away dead tissue plus strong antibiotics.
takeaway: Gas bubbles tracking through the muscles is gas gangrene a surgical emergency.

symptom: a baby with a slowly growing belly mass since birth
hook: a small malformed twin skeleton was hidden inside the abdomen of the baby
view: AP upper abdomen of an infant cropped at the navel with no pelvis or groin in frame
keyFindings: a small malformed mass within the infant upper abdomen containing recognisable fetal type bones such as a curved spine and limb bones representing an enclosed undeveloped twin
whatYouSee: A tiny malformed skeleton hidden inside the infant abdomen.
whyItMatters: It is a rare anomaly where an undeveloped twin is enclosed within the body of the other twin and is removed surgically.
treatment: It is removed with surgery and the child usually does well.
takeaway: Recognisable fetal bones inside an infant abdomen is a fetus in fetu.

symptom: a vague pressure in the chest and a cough they kept blaming on a chest infection
hook: there was a fully formed human tooth sitting in a mass right in the middle of the chest
view: AP chest radiograph centered on the mediastinum between the lungs (one view filling the frame, no pelvis or groin)
keyFindings: an anterior mediastinal soft tissue mass between the lungs containing ONE large unmistakable fully formed human tooth shaped exactly like a real molar with a bright white crown and clear roots standing out sharply against the darker mass so it is obviously a tooth in the chest
whatYouSee: A mass in the middle of the chest with a real human tooth inside it.
whyItMatters: A teratoma grows from stray germ cells and can contain teeth hair and bone and is removed surgically.
treatment: It is removed with surgery and is usually cured once it is taken out.
takeaway: A chest mass that contains a tooth is a mediastinal teratoma.

aliases: gas cysts in the bowel wall
symptom: ordinary bloating and a bit too much gas after meals that they just lived with
hook: the wall of the bowel was lined with rows of bubbles like a strip of bubble wrap
view: AP abdomen radiograph cropped at the navel showing the bowel with no pelvis or groin in frame
keyFindings: multiple small lucent gas cysts and linear streaks of gas lining the wall of the bowel loops giving a bubbly beaded bubble-wrap appearance
whatYouSee: The bowel wall lined with rows of little gas bubbles like bubble wrap.
whyItMatters: Gas in the bowel wall can be harmless or a sign of dying bowel so it always needs checking.
treatment: Mild cases are watched while a sick bowel needs urgent surgery.
takeaway: Bubbles lining the bowel wall is pneumatosis intestinalis.

symptom: what felt like a stubborn urine infection and a dull ache in the side that would not quit
hook: the kidney was puffed up and fizzing with gas like a slice of half melted Swiss cheese
view: AP abdomen radiograph of the flank and kidney cropped at the navel with no pelvis or groin in frame
keyFindings: mottled and streaky lucent gas bubbling through and around the swollen kidney in the flank giving a foamy Swiss-cheese pattern over the renal shadow
whatYouSee: A kidney bubbling and fizzing with gas like Swiss cheese.
whyItMatters: Gas forming bacteria are destroying the kidney from inside and it can kill fast without urgent treatment.
treatment: It needs urgent antibiotics drainage and sometimes removal of the kidney.
takeaway: Gas bubbling through the kidney is emphysematous pyelonephritis.

symptom: a little cough that lingered after a cold so they got a routine chest film
hook: a round lump in the lung was studded with chunky white flecks like a handful of popcorn turned to stone
view: PA chest radiograph showing the lungs (one view, no pelvis or groin)
keyFindings: a large well defined round lung nodule densely packed with chunky clustered popcorn-like calcifications standing out bright white so it clearly looks like a clump of popcorn turned to stone sitting in the lung
whatYouSee: A round lump in the lung full of popcorn made of stone.
whyItMatters: It is a harmless benign tumour but the popcorn pattern helps tell it apart from cancer.
treatment: It is usually just watched and only removed if it grows or causes trouble.
takeaway: A lung nodule with popcorn calcification is a hamartoma.

symptom: a swollen gum they blamed on a bad wisdom tooth
hook: the jawbone was blown out from inside into a cluster of soap bubbles
view: panoramic radiograph of the mandible (one view, no pelvis or groin)
keyFindings: a multilocular expansile lucent lesion blowing out the mandible into a soap-bubble pattern of rounded loculations
whatYouSee: A jawbone ballooned from inside into soap bubbles.
whyItMatters: It is a benign but locally aggressive tumour that keeps growing and destroying the jaw if left.
treatment: It is removed with surgery and the jaw is often reconstructed.
takeaway: A multilocular soap bubble lesion of the jaw is an ameloblastoma.

symptom: slowly creeping breathlessness over years that felt like just getting unfit
hook: both lungs were filled top to bottom with countless tiny dense grains like a bag of sand poured into the chest
view: PA chest radiograph showing both lungs (one view, no pelvis or groin)
keyFindings: innumerable tiny dense micronodules filling both lungs from top to bottom giving a fine sandstorm-like whiteout
whatYouSee: Lungs filled with countless tiny grains like sand.
whyItMatters: Tiny stones build up in the air sacs over years and slowly stiffen the lungs.
treatment: There is no cure and severe cases may need a lung transplant.
takeaway: Lungs full of tiny sand-like stones is alveolar microlithiasis.

symptom: swollen ankles and getting puffed on the stairs that they put down to age
hook: a hard white shell of calcium was wrapped right around the heart like an eggshell
view: lateral chest radiograph showing the heart (one view, no pelvis or groin)
keyFindings: a curvilinear dense white rim of calcification encasing the heart like a shell
whatYouSee: The heart sealed inside a hard eggshell of calcium.
whyItMatters: The stiff calcified shell stops the heart filling properly and causes heart failure.
treatment: The thickened shell is peeled off the heart with surgery.
takeaway: A calcium shell around the heart is constrictive pericarditis.

symptom: vague belly cramps and just feeling off like a stomach bug that would pass
hook: the liver was veined through with branching dark channels of air like a bolt of lightning frozen inside it
view: AP abdomen radiograph of the upper abdomen and liver cropped at the navel with no pelvis or groin
keyFindings: branching tubular lucent gas reaching out to the edge of the liver like a tree or lightning pattern
whatYouSee: Branching air spreading through the liver like lightning.
whyItMatters: Air in the liver veins often means the bowel is dying and is a medical emergency.
treatment: It usually needs urgent surgery to treat the dying bowel behind it.
takeaway: Branching gas reaching the edge of the liver is portal venous gas.

symptom: a grumbling ache in the upper belly after eating that got brushed off as indigestion
hook: the pancreas was dusted edge to edge with tiny white stones like gravel scattered across the upper belly
view: AP abdomen radiograph of the upper abdomen cropped at the navel with no pelvis or groin
keyFindings: multiple coarse and punctate calcifications scattered across the pancreas in the upper abdomen like a band of gravel
whatYouSee: The pancreas scattered with tiny stones like gravel.
whyItMatters: It is the hallmark of a pancreas scarred by years of inflammation often from alcohol.
treatment: Care is pain control enzyme tablets and stopping alcohol.
takeaway: Gravel-like calcification across the pancreas is chronic pancreatitis.

symptom: a dry nagging cough and a bit of breathlessness they blamed on being unfit
hook: a huge round water balloon was sitting in the lung and the burst skin floated on the fluid like a water lily
view: PA chest radiograph showing the lung (one view, no pelvis or groin)
keyFindings: a large round cyst in the lung with an air-fluid level and a crumpled floating membrane giving the water-lily sign
whatYouSee: A water balloon in the lung with a lily floating on top.
whyItMatters: It is a tapeworm cyst grown in the lung and bursting it can cause a severe allergic reaction.
treatment: It is removed with surgery along with antiparasitic medicine.
takeaway: A lung cyst with a floating membrane is a hydatid cyst.

symptom: a puffy cheek they figured was a sinus infection that would not clear
hook: the facial bones had turned to frosted glass and swelled out like rising bread dough
view: Waters view of the facial bones and skull (one view, no pelvis or groin)
keyFindings: expansile hazy ground-glass bone replacing and swelling the facial bones and skull
whatYouSee: Facial bones turned to frosted glass and swollen like dough.
whyItMatters: Normal bone is replaced by weak fibrous tissue that swells and deforms the face.
treatment: Care is supportive with surgery to reshape disabling overgrowth.
takeaway: Ground-glass swelling of the facial bones is fibrous dysplasia.

symptom: a firm spot on the gum they kept poking with their tongue
hook: the jaw was filling up with flecks like a snow flurry frozen inside the bone
view: panoramic radiograph of the jaw (one view, no pelvis or groin)
keyFindings: a lucent jaw lesion scattered with radiopaque flecks giving a driven-snow appearance
whatYouSee: A jaw filling with flecks like frozen snow.
whyItMatters: It is a rare jaw tumour that slowly expands the bone and needs removing.
treatment: It is removed with surgery.
takeaway: A jaw lesion with driven-snow flecks is a Pindborg tumour.
